questions.specification: The function of silencer DNA sequences is to:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the function of silencer DNA sequences, which act as binding sites for repressor proteins to inhibit or decrease transcription. This is a core part of the regulation of gene expression via protein-DNA interactions.

options.content: Increase transcription rates
Inhibit transcription when bound by repressors
Bind RNA polymerase to initiate transcription
Stabilize mRNA in the cytoplasm

questions.specification: Which of the following describes the function of the A site of a ribosome during the process of translation?

options.content: It is the site where the tRNA is released from the ribosome after donating its amino acid.
It holds the tRNA molecule that is attached to the growing polypeptide chain.
It acts as the binding site for the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA molecule.
It is the region where the small ribosomal subunit first binds to the mRNA leader sequence.

questions.specification: Epigenetic mechanisms can regulate gene expression in development, physiology and responses to the environment.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.13 Effects of the hormones oestradiol and progesterone on target cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) specifically asks for the mechanism by which oestrogen (oestradiol) regulates gene expression in target cells (endometrium), which is the direct focus of this topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.9 Consequences of removal of most but not all epigenetic tags from the ovum and sperm
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.9 Consequences of removal of most but not all epigenetic tags from the ovum and sperm
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) concerns genomic imprinting, which is defined in the curriculum as the consequence of not all epigenetic tags being removed from the ovum and sperm, specifically influencing phenotype (size) in hybrids.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.11 External factors impacting the pattern of gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.11 External factors impacting the pattern of gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) addresses how environmental factors (pollutants) influence the pattern of gene expression through epigenetic regulation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.1 Reproduction > D3.1.5 Changes during the ovarian and uterine cycles and their hormonal regulation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.1.5 Changes during the ovarian and uterine cycles and their hormonal regulation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) is set within the context of the uterine cycle and its hormonal regulation, which this topic covers.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.12 Intracellular receptors that affect gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.12 Intracellular receptors that affect gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) involves the mechanism of intracellular receptors (for oestrogen) that affect gene expression.

parts.content: Outline the consequences of genomic imprinting in lion–tiger hybrids, explaining the origin of size differences between ligers and tigons.
parts.markscheme:
- genomic imprinting is the epigenetic silencing of specific genes according to the parent of origin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- it involves DNA methylation and/or histone modification during gamete formation/early development <marks>1 mark</marks>
- paternally expressed alleles often promote fetal growth while maternally expressed alleles often limit growth to conserve maternal resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ligers (lion father, tiger mother) can express paternally derived growth-promoting alleles without equivalent maternally derived growth-inhibiting effects, leading to larger size <marks>1 mark</marks>
- tigons (tiger father, lion mother) have reduced expression of growth-promoting alleles and/or stronger growth-inhibiting maternal effects, leading to smaller size <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how environmental pollutants, such as air pollution, can influence the epigenetic regulation of immune response genes.
parts.markscheme:
- pollutants can change DNA methylation patterns in somatic cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hypermethylation of promoter regions typically reduces transcription by preventing transcription factor binding <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hypomethylation can increase transcription and contribute to chronic inflammation/autoimmunity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- pollutants can alter histone modifications (e.g. acetylation/methylation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- histone changes modify chromatin packing/accessibility and so alter transcription of cytokine/immune genes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the mechanism by which oestrogen regulates gene expression related to endometrial development and maintenance in the uterine cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- oestrogen is a lipid-soluble steroid hormone that diffuses across the plasma membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- oestrogen binds to a specific intracellular oestrogen receptor (cytoplasmic and/or nuclear) causing receptor activation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the hormone–receptor complex dimerises and is transported to/acts in the nucleus <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the complex binds to specific DNA sequences (oestrogen response elements) near target genes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- binding recruits co-activators and/or modifies chromatin (e.g. histone acetylation), increasing transcription initiation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- mRNA is produced and translated into proteins that promote endometrial cell proliferation, vascularisation and thickening <marks>1 mark</marks>
- reduced oestrogen later in the cycle decreases receptor-mediated transcription, contributing to endometrial breakdown if implantation does not occur <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
In the image below, the left panel shows changes in global DNA methylation levels (%) in somatic cells over three generations ($F_0$ to $F_3$) following an environmental stimulus in the $F_0$ generation. The right panel displays the expression level of a phenotypic trait over six generations ($F_0$ to $F_5$), highlighting how the phenotype changes across generations despite no changes in the DNA sequence.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/da77e3be-0e2f-4fa7-ba76-cf810f699539.png)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b–e) focus on changes in global DNA methylation, methylation-mediated gene silencing, and promoter methylation as an epigenetic tag affecting transcription.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.7 Epigenetic inheritance through heritable changes to gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.7 Epigenetic inheritance through heritable changes to gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly tracks methylation and phenotype across multiple generations without DNA sequence change, i.e., transgenerational/epigenetic inheritance of gene expression states.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.8 Examples of environmental effects on gene expression in cells and organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.8 Examples of environmental effects on gene expression in cells and organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: An environmental stimulus in F0 leading to altered methylation and phenotype across generations matches environmental effects on gene expression/epigenetic states.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.2 Inheritance > D3.2.6 Phenotypic plasticity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.2.6 Phenotypic plasticity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks how epigenetic changes contribute to phenotypic plasticity without altering DNA sequence.

parts.content: State the trend in global DNA methylation (%) from $F_0$ to $F_3$.
parts.markscheme: - DNA methylation levels decrease from $F_0$ to $F_3$ (gradual loss of methylation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify the generation in which the phenotypic trait expression peaks. Suggest a possible epigenetic explanation for why expression peaks at that generation.
parts.markscheme:
- Phenotypic trait expression peaks in generation $F_2$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced DNA methylation can lead to transient gene activation / loss of silencing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Peak could reflect the greatest extent of epigenetic change (largest net demethylation) occurring by $F_2$ before partial re-methylation/resetting in later generations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Changes in chromatin state associated with demethylation (euchromatin more accessible) can increase transcription temporarily <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how DNA methylation can be involved in the heritable silencing of gene expression.
parts.markscheme:
- Methylation of cytosines (often in CpG sites) in promoter regions can reduce transcription <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Methyl groups can block binding of transcription factors/RNA polymerase to promoter DNA <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Methylated DNA can recruit methyl-CpG-binding proteins that recruit histone deacetylases and/or other repressors, leading to chromatin condensation (heterochromatin) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintenance methyltransferases copy methylation patterns after DNA replication so the silenced state can be inherited through cell divisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how epigenetic changes, such as those shown here, can contribute to phenotypic plasticity without altering the DNA sequence.
parts.markscheme:
- Environmental stimuli (e.g., diet, toxins, stress) can alter epigenetic marks such as DNA methylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Altered methylation changes gene expression patterns, producing different phenotypes without changing the base sequence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some epigenetic patterns can be transmitted to daughter cells and/or across generations, affecting offspring phenotype <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Epigenetic marks can be reversible/dynamic, allowing phenotypes to shift as conditions change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different levels of methylation across generations can explain a rise-and-fall in trait expression even when genotype is unchanged <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Figure showing transcriptional regulation of gene $A$ in a eukaryotic cell.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/JVSDpoTSLTU4QHhUZGXGA.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on eukaryotic transcriptional regulation via promoter, enhancer, activators, DNA bending proteins, transcription factors, mediator proteins, and RNA polymerase—core regulation of transcription by proteins.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D1.2 Protein synthesis > D1.2.13 Initiation of transcription at the promoter (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D1.2.13 Initiation of transcription at the promoter (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts ask specifically for the function of the promoter and how transcription is initiated at the promoter through assembly/interactions of factors and RNA polymerase.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The final part explicitly targets how this mechanism enables differential gene expression in different cell types, i.e., gene expression control.

parts.content: State the function of the promoter shown in the figure.
parts.markscheme: - Promoter is the DNA sequence where RNA polymerase binds to begin transcription of a gene. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of (i) transcription factors and (ii) mediator proteins in this diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- Transcription factors bind to specific DNA sequences at/near the promoter. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transcription factors help recruit and/or correctly position RNA polymerase at the promoter to form an initiation complex. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mediator proteins interact with transcription factors and RNA polymerase as part of the transcription initiation complex. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mediator proteins act as a link/bridge transmitting the effect of activators at distal control elements to the promoter-bound transcription machinery. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the enhancer and its associated proteins regulate the transcription of gene $A$.
parts.markscheme:
- Specific activator proteins bind to the enhancer (distal control elements). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- DNA-bending/looping brings the enhancer region into physical proximity with the promoter. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Activators interact (directly or via mediator) with transcription factors/RNA polymerase to increase transcription initiation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This interaction stabilizes assembly of the transcription initiation complex at the promoter. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases the rate/frequency of transcription of gene $A$ (more mRNA produced). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one reason why this regulatory mechanism allows differential gene expression in different cell types.
parts.markscheme:
- Different cell types express different transcriptional regulators (e.g. activator proteins). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhancers respond only to specific activators, so gene $A$ is transcribed only in cells where the required activator(s) are present/active. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In cell types lacking the activator(s) (or with repressors present), the initiation complex is not stabilized and transcription remains low/off. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It is transcribed into the non-coding leader sequence of the pre-mRNA.
It acts as the binding site for the small ribosomal subunit.
It serves as a template for the addition of the 5' methyl-guanosine cap.

questions.specification:
The figure shows how pre-pregnancy parental factors, such as genetic background and lifestyle exposures (e.g., diet, stress, pollutants) can lead to epigenetic modifications - including DNA methylation, histone modification, and changes in non-coding RNAs - in germ cells (sperm or egg) of the F0 generation. These epigenetic tags are retained during meiosis, meaning they can be passed to offspring (F1) and even the second generation (F2), creating transgenerational epigenetic inheritance without changes to the underlying DNA sequence. ![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ZJvZsufznWImyeDM8TqJb.jpeg) Recent research compared methylation patterns in families exposed to high air pollution (Group A) and low pollution (Group B). Table 1 summarizes the frequency of methylated promoter regions detected in blood cells of children (F1 generation). **Table 1: Frequency of promoter methylation in F1 children from exposed vs. unexposed families** | Group | Sample Size
(n) | % of Genes With Methylated
Promoters | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | High Pollution (A) | 30 | 45% | | Low Pollution (B) | 30 | 20% |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.7 Epigenetic inheritance through heritable changes to gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.7 Epigenetic inheritance through heritable changes to gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question centers on transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, explaining how modifications are passed across generations (F1 to F2) without changing the DNA sequence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically identifies DNA methylation of the promoter and histone modifications as the mechanisms for these epigenetic changes.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.8 Examples of environmental effects on gene expression in cells and organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.8 Examples of environmental effects on gene expression in cells and organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The context of the question involves air pollution as an environmental factor affecting gene expression and asks for other environmental examples like diet or stress.

parts.content: Calculate the difference in promoter methylation between Group A and Group B using Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
$45\% - 20\% = 25\%$ difference 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the figure, explain how histone modifications can contribute to transgenerational inheritance without changing the DNA sequence.
parts.markscheme:
- Histone modifications (e.g., acetylation/methylation) alter chromatin packing (euchromatin vs heterochromatin), changing access of transcription machinery <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This changes gene expression (upregulation or silencing) without altering the base sequence of DNA <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some histone marks can escape erasure/reprogramming during gametogenesis/early development and therefore be transmitted via germ cells to offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inherited chromatin state can influence developmental gene expression patterns/phenotype in the next generation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify one phenotypic effect that increased promoter methylation in Group A children might have on cellular function.
parts.markscheme: Reduced expression of certain genes $\rightarrow$ possible altered metabolism, stress responses, or disease susceptibility <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why F2 is the first generation classified as "transgenerational epigenetic inheritance" in this system, as shown in the figure.
parts.markscheme:
- F0 exposure can affect F0 germ cells (sperm/eggs) that form the F1 generation, so F1 is not considered unexposed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During pregnancy, an F1 embryo and its developing germ cells (future F2) can also be directly exposed to the same environmental factor <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore, F2 is the first generation where observed epigenetic effects can be due to inheritance rather than direct exposure (i.e., transmitted through the germ line) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transgenerational implies persistence after the exposure has ceased across at least one generation not directly exposed <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one environmental example (besides air pollution) that could alter epigenetic tags, and describe its potential effect on gene expression.
parts.markscheme:
- Example: diet (e.g., folate/methyl-donor intake), cigarette smoke, chronic stress, alcohol, endocrine disruptors/pesticides <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can change DNA methylation and/or histone modification patterns, altering transcription of target genes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased promoter methylation typically represses transcription; decreased promoter methylation may increase transcription <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Histone acetylation generally increases transcription by loosening chromatin; deacetylation generally decreases transcription <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: The genome consists of all RNA; the transcriptome contains only proteins
The genome includes all genetic material; the transcriptome includes all expressed RNA transcripts
The transcriptome consists of proteins; the genome includes only expressed genes
The genome and transcriptome are identical in all cells

In a biological context, epigenesis refers to the process by which an embryo develops through a sequence of steps where cells differentiate and form organs, as opposed to the theory of preformation.

options.content: The formation of proteins from mRNA
The development of differentiation patterns in a multicellular organism
The transcription and translation of genes
The replication of DNA sequences

questions.specification:
A study examined the expression of the $\beta$-galactosidase enzyme in *E. coli* under different conditions. The enzyme is encoded by the *lacZ* gene, which is part of the *lac* operon, a gene regulatory system in bacteria. The results are shown in the table below:

| Condition | Lactose Present? | Glucose Present? | $\beta$-Galactosidase Activity (relative units, RU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | No | Yes | 0 |
| B | No | No | 0 |
| C | Yes | Yes | 10 |
| D | Yes | No | 100 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.2 Regulation of transcription by proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about lac operon control of transcription via regulatory proteins (lac repressor, CAP-cAMP) responding to lactose/glucose conditions, explaining differences in β-galactosidase expression.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: More generally targets prokaryotic gene expression levels under different environmental conditions (induction/repression of an operon), beyond the specifics of transcription-factor regulation.

parts.content: Explain why $\beta$-galactosidase activity is higher in condition D than in condition C.
parts.markscheme:
- Lactose (present in both C and D) acts as an inducer (allolactose) by binding to the LacI repressor so it cannot bind the operator; transcription can occur <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In condition D (no glucose), cAMP levels are high, so cAMP binds CAP; the CAP–cAMP complex binds near the promoter and increases RNA polymerase binding/transcription of the *lac* operon <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In condition C (glucose present), cAMP is low so CAP does not bind; reduced transcription (catabolite repression) so lower $\beta$-galactosidase activity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Expression of *lacZ* (and other structural genes such as *lacY*/*lacA*) increases when CAP–cAMP is bound, leading to higher enzyme levels in D <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the *lac* operon regulates gene expression in response to lactose.
parts.markscheme:
- In the absence of lactose, the LacI repressor binds to the operator, preventing RNA polymerase from transcribing the structural genes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the presence of lactose (allolactose), the inducer binds to the repressor causing a conformational change so it cannot bind the operator; transcription proceeds <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The operon includes a promoter and operator controlling transcription of structural genes such as *lacZ*, *lacY* and *lacA* <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This is negative regulation: lactose removes repression rather than directly activating transcription <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What is the biological effect of acetylating lysine residues on histone tails in a nucleosome?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on the acetylation of histone tails within nucleosomes, which is a specific form of chemical modification that acts as an epigenetic tag to regulate gene expression by altering chromatin accessibility. This falls directly under the study of epigenetic tags on histones.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.2 Gene expression (HL) > D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.1 Gene expression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Histone acetylation is a primary mechanism for the regulation of gene expression, determining whether the DNA is accessible for transcription or remains silenced in condensed chromatin.

options.content: It increases chromatin condensation, preventing transcription.
options.markscheme: Histone acetylation generally reduces histone–DNA attraction and is associated with euchromatin, not increased condensation.

options.content: It neutralizes positive charges on histones, making DNA more accessible for transcription.
options.markscheme: Acetylation of lysines reduces the positive charge on histone tails, weakening interaction with negatively charged DNA and increasing accessibility for transcription.

options.content: It signals the spliceosome to remove specific introns from pre-mRNA.
options.markscheme: Spliceosome activity is part of RNA processing and is not a direct effect of histone acetylation in chromatin.

options.content: It promotes binding of repressor proteins to silencer sequences.
options.markscheme: Histone acetylation is typically associated with transcriptional activation rather than promoting repressor binding to silencers.

questions.specification:

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/z72xLPTXS3dAP3bxE9buO.jpeg)

**Figure B** illustrates early stages of embryonic development and epigenetic regulation during the maternal-to-zygotic transition (MZT).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.6 Methylation of the promoter and histones in nucleosomes as examples of epigenetic tags

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.2.9 Consequences of removal of most but not all epigenetic tags from the ovum and sperm
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Figure B references maternal-to-zygotic transition and early embryo epigenetic reprogramming; this aligns with removal/retention of epigenetic tags between gametes/early embryo stages.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.1 Reproduction > D3.1.16 Development of a blastocyst and implantation in the endometrium (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.1.16 Development of a blastocyst and implantation in the endometrium (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Figure B contrasts pre-implantation and post-implantation stages; this links to early embryo development stages including blastocyst/implantation timing context (even though emphasis is epigenetic regulation).

parts.content: Using **Figure A**, identify two molecular components required for Cas9 to recognize and cut DNA.
parts.markscheme:
- Cas9 protein <marks>1 mark</marks>
- guide RNA (sgRNA or crRNA + tracrRNA complex) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using **Figure A**, compare the two DNA repair mechanisms (HDR and NHEJ) and explain how each can result in different outcomes for the cell.
parts.markscheme:
- HDR uses a homologous donor DNA template to repair the break <marks>1 mark</marks>
- HDR is relatively precise and can allow targeted correction/insertion of sequences (knock-in) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- NHEJ repairs without a template by directly ligating the broken ends <marks>1 mark</marks>
- NHEJ is error-prone and often produces insertions/deletions (indels), potentially causing frameshifts/gene knockout <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using **Figure B**, describe the significance of the zygotic genome activation (ZGA) and state when it occurs during embryonic development.
parts.markscheme:
- ZGA marks the shift from maternal control (stored maternal mRNA/proteins) to transcription from the embryo’s own genome <marks>1 mark</marks>
- allows new gene expression needed for continued development/cell fate decisions as maternal transcripts are removed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- timing: minor ZGA occurs around the 2-cell stage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- timing: major ZGA begins around the 8-cell stage/morula <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using **Figure B**, discuss how DNA methylation changes influence gene expression between the pre-implantation and post-implantation stages.
parts.markscheme:
- Pre-implantation: genome-wide demethylation/hypomethylation occurs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hypomethylation is associated with a more relaxed/open chromatin state and increased transcriptional accessibility (supports pluripotency/gene activation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Post-implantation: de novo methylation leads to overall higher methylation/hypermethylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hypermethylation is associated with more condensed chromatin and reduced transcription/silencing of genes to restrict cell fate (differentiation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one way the CRISPR-Cas9 system could be used to study gene function during embryonic development.
parts.markscheme:
Knock out a specific gene using NHEJ and observe resulting developmental phenotype/defects <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

Use HDR to insert a reporter (e.g., GFP) at a gene locus to track gene expression patterns/cell lineage <marks>1 mark</marks>
